data.title: Asian equities retreat as hawkish Fed cues, energy volatility, and mixed tech earnings sour sentiment
data.description:
Asian equities traded mostly lower on Thursday as investors weighed a sharp surge in oil prices, mixed corporate earnings, and a cautious policy outlook from the Federal Reserve.

Gold prices traded around $4,550 an ounce.

WTI crude futures climbed above $108 per barrel, rallying nearly 15% so far this week.

The benchmark KOSPI edged down 0.1% to around 6,680.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 1.36% to around 59,300, while the broader Topix Index fell 1.6% to 3,710 in post-holiday trading. The Japanese yen weakened past the critical 160 per dollar threshold on Thursday, reaching its lowest level since July 2024.

Retail sales in Japan rose by 1.7% year-over-year in March 2026, rebounding from a revised 0.1% decline in February and exceeding market predictions of 0.8%. Meanwhile, industrial production fell by 0.5% month-on-month, below the 1.1% expected, marking a second consecutive decline due to external challenges.

China's (SHCOMP) rose 0.12% to 4,110, remaining at its strongest level in over a month, while the Shenzhen Composite rose 0.3% to 15,167, and the offshore yuan traded around 6.84 per dollar, halting its recent decline and on track for monthly gains as stronger-than-expected PMI data.

China’s official NBS Manufacturing PMI slightly decreased to 50.3 in April 2026 from March's 50.4, exceeding expectations of 50.1. Conversely, China’s NBS Non-Manufacturing PMI dropped to 49.4 in April from 50.1 in March, falling short of the expected 49.9.

However, the RatingDog China General Manufacturing PMI rose to 52.2, up from 50.8, marking the fastest growth since December 2020.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.25% to 25,947.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.40%  to 76,677, reversing the previous session’s gains as sentiment weakened amid a sharp surge in crude oil prices. The Indian rupee fell to around 95.1 per dollar, marking a fresh record low as mounting external pressures continued to strain currency markets.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.24% to 8,641 in Thursday’s morning session, marking an eighth straight decline and a three-week low.

Australia's export prices rose by 0.5% in Q1 2026, down from 3.2% in Q4 2025. Import prices increased by 0.1% in Q1 2026, despite a predicted decline.

Australia’s private sector credit increased by 0.7% month-on-month in March 2026, coming in slightly above market expectations and February’s 0.6% rise. 

In the U.S. on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended lower on Wednesday after the Federal Reserve held rates unchanged and presented a split outlook, while markets positioned ahead of hyperscaler earnings.

U.S. stock futures climbed as investors assessed quarterly results from the Magnificent Seven technology firms: Dow -0.69%; S&P 500 -0.37%; Nasdaq -0.39%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Nvidia’s NVentures invests in Swedish AI legal tech Legora at $5.6B valuation, CNBC reports
data.description:
Nvidia’s (NVDA) venture arm, NVentures, has invested in Swedish AI legal tech Legora at a $5.6B valuation, as the chip giant continues to ramp up startup funding across the globe.

Legora on Thursday exclusively told CNBC that Nvidia had backed it as part of a $50M extension of its Series D, which brought the total amount to $600M following a first close in March. The extension also saw Atlassian, Adams Street Partners and Insight participate.

Nvidia (NVDA) has been ramping up startup investments in recent years, as its looked to deepen its ties to some of the world’s most promising companies, offering technical expertise and supply chain assistance, alongside hard cash.

Legora is its first bet in the legal tech sector, according to Dealroom data. The size of its investment was not disclosed, CNBC reported.

The AI startup is building AI agents and tools to help lawyers automate and streamline workflows. The company launched an ad campaign this month featuring the actor Jude Law, using the slogan: “Law just got more attractive.”

data.title: SoftBank targets $100B IPO for AI robotics venture Roze
data.description:
Japan’s SoftBank Group (SFTBY) is planning to create a new AI and robotics company designed to automate data center construction, with an eye on a roughly $100B U.S. IPO, FT reported, citing sources.

The Japanese conglomerate plans for Roze to go public as early as this year, as founder Masayoshi Son accelerates efforts to advance the group’s AI strategy.

SoftBank (SFTBY) plans to hold an analyst day in a data center facility in Texas in July to promote the IPO, the report added. The group has yet to determine the size of the stake it will sell in Roze, though it has historically retained majority ownership in its listed companies. SoftBank, for instance, continues to hold nearly 90% of Arm Holdings (ARM) following its 2023 listing.

The valuation target and IPO timeline remain subject to change, with some SoftBank (SFTBY) executives viewing the plans as ambitious amid ongoing uncertainty linked to the Middle East conflict.

Roze is the Japanese group’s planned AI and robotics venture aiming to deploy AI-driven systems and robots to speed up construction and improve efficiency, addressing a key bottleneck in AI infrastructure as demand for computing capacity surges.

data.title: Equinix raises 2026 total revenue growth outlook to 10%-11% while shifting Hampton xScale economics into Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Equinix (EQIX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “This quarter's results reflect continued strength across the business as we capitalize on a large and growing set of opportunities,” said Adaire Fox-Martin (President, CEO & Director), adding that “demand is broad-based and durable” and that “AI continues to fuel infrastructure investments that play to our strengths.”
  • Fox-Martin said “our recurring revenue grew 10% on a normalized and constant currency basis” and called Q1 “the largest quarter of total sales activity in our history,” with “total sales activity… up more than 35% year-over-year,” alongside “reducing churn.”
  • On AI positioning, Fox-Martin said “8 of the top 10 AI model providers and 4 of the top 5 neoclouds are actively expanding with Equinix,” and that they “have placed more than 110 separate network nodes with us,” adding “approximately 60% of our largest deals in Q1 were AI related.”
  • On product expansion, Fox-Martin said “The Equinix Distributed AI Hub… gives enterprises a single private low-latency connection to the entire AI ecosystem,” and said “Equinix Fabric Intelligence… [is] built directly into our fabric interconnection platform.”
  • On M&A, Fox-Martin said Equinix signed “a joint agreement with Canada Pension Plan Investment Board to purchase atNorth,” with “approximately 800 megawatts expected to come online over the next 5 years,” and said it is “expected to be immediately accretive to AFFO per share upon closing.”
  • Olivier Leonetti (Chief Financial Officer) said “recurring revenues were $2.3 billion, up 10% year-over-year,” “total revenues were $2.4 billion, up 8% year-over-year,” and “adjusted EBITDA was $1.2 billion… resulting in a 51% adjusted EBITDA margin.” He added that “quarterly AFFO surpassed the $1 billion mark for the first time… and AFFO per share was $10.79.”

Outlook

  • Leonetti said Equinix is “raising guidance across key financial metrics,” and for Q2 expects “MRR growth of 10% to 11% year-over-year.”
  • On the Hampton xScale lease timing, Leonetti said “we have adjusted the expected timing,” and that “the expanded economics are now included in our guidance for Q2,” adding, “This timing shift does not impact our full year outlook because the economics were already incorporated.”
  • For full-year 2026, Leonetti said Equinix is “raising total revenue guidance by $21 million… improving expected total revenue growth range… to 10% to 11%,” “raising adjusted EBITDA guidance by $24 million,” and “raising AFFO guidance by approximately $40 million,” which “corresponds to… AFFO per share growth range to 9% to 11%.”
  • On investment pace, Leonetti said “excluding xScale and land acquisitions, we now expect total capital expenditures to approximate the top end of our prior range at $4.1 billion,” and added the team is “evaluate[ing] opportunities to accelerate our capacity.”

Financial Results

  • Leonetti said “churn came at 1.7%,” and said Equinix is “tracking towards the low end of our 2% to 2.5% guidance range,” while “MRR per cabinet increased to $2,524.”
  • He said Equinix “increased physical and virtual net interconnections by 5,800,” “added 4,100 net cabinets billing,” and that “our backlog of cabinets sold but not yet installed is at a record level.”
  • On interconnection monetization, Fox-Martin said “total interconnection revenue was up 9% year-over-year,” “fabric revenue growth” was “26%,” and “fabric bookings were up 70% year-over-year.”
  • On capital structure and funding, Leonetti said Equinix had “$3.1 billion of cash and short-term investments,” “net leverage was 3.8x,” and said it “issued $1.5 billion of senior notes at a blended effective rate of 3.1%.”
  • On spending and returns, Leonetti said Q1 “total capital expenditures… were about $1.3 billion” and “we continue to expect mid 20% unlevered cash-on-cash returns on investment.”
  • On energy exposure, Leonetti said Equinix is “more than 90% hedged for 2026” and expects “minimum impact for 2026, even if energy prices were to remain elevated.”

Q&A

  • Michael Rollins, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked whether the “110” AI ecosystem nodes were incremental and what demand looks like; President Fox-Martin replied the nodes are “in addition to all of the nodes… being deployed by the hyperscalers,” and described demand for “connectivity to the CSPs and the NSPs,” “AI inference nodes for densely populated metros,” and “fabric access to the enterprise customer base.”
  • Cameron McVeigh, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: asked about “$140 million in pre-leasing activity” and terms; President Fox-Martin said “pricing remains firm” and presales provides “security… in terms of the infrastructure that they're defining.”
  • Matthew Niknam, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division: asked if energy and IT cost inflation is changing behavior; President Fox-Martin said “we're not certainly seeing any pullback from customers” and reiterated “a very durable and broad-based demand environment.”
  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked if AI inferencing changes incremental capex and returns; President Fox-Martin said “we don't see any difference in the capital,” SVP Phillip Konieczny said “the densities… are much higher,” and Konieczny added returns remain “in that mid-20s percent.”
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Research Division: asked about sequential bookings seasonality and interconnection; President Fox-Martin said Q1 is “seasonally… lower” but was “our largest Q1 ever,” and said interconnection growth is supported by solutions “in pre-preview with a number of customers.”
  • Jonathan Atkin, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: asked if interconnection can outgrow overall revenue; President Fox-Martin said there is “potential for upside here, but that is not yet factored into our plans.”
  • Jyhhaw Liu, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked about Middle East operations; President Fox-Martin said facilities are “fully operational,” the region is “about 1% of total revenues,” and DX3’s “RFS date… be impacted due to the conflict.”
  • Nicholas Del Deo, MoffettNathanson LLC: asked CFO capital allocation philosophy; CFO Leonetti said “we want first to use debt,” and “We will use equity on an opportunistic basis.”
  • Richard Choe, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked if 1.7% churn reverses; President Fox-Martin said “we don't want to call victory too early” and “keep our churn in the range of 2% to 2.5% for the rest of the year.”
  • David Guarino, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: asked about xScale one-time fees and Manuka; President Fox-Martin said Manuka is “not timing that we have put into the short term,” and CFO Leonetti said “the rest of the xScale deals are relatively small in nature.”
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about cloud repatriation and edge decisioning; President Fox-Martin said it is “a hybrid multi-cloud environment” and “I wouldn't say that this is a broad-based conversation.”
  • Madison Rezaei, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: asked whether $4.1 billion capex is the new run-rate and return durability; President Fox-Martin said Equinix has “3 gigawatts currently either in land under control or in development,” and CFO Leonetti said “The mid 25% is a target. That's not an aspiration.”
  • Erik Rasmussen, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: asked about liquid cooling; President Fox-Martin said “36 deployments” are active, with “7 orders within Q1… up 50% Q-on-Q.”
  • Joseph Osha, Guggenheim Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about edge constraints; President Fox-Martin said “the availability of power would be the largest constraint.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with recurring diligence on AI monetization, presales commitments, churn sustainability, geopolitics, and xScale fee cadence, including questions like “how tenant appetite is changing” (Morgan Stanley) and whether churn stays low given “some of it is delayed” (JPMorgan).
  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and largely steady in Q&A, with repeated “pricing remains firm” (President Fox-Martin) and confidence statements like “we are raising guidance across key financial metrics” (CFO Leonetti), while using caution on select topics: “we don't want to call victory too early” (President Fox-Martin) and “not timing that we have put into the short term” (President Fox-Martin) on Manuka.
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a similarly upbeat stance on AI demand; the main tonal change was more specificity on AI ecosystem buildout (nodes/neoclouds/model providers) and more explicit commentary on constraints (“availability of power”) and regional disruption (DX3 RFS impact).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership and messaging shifted with CFO succession: Q4 included “Keith's retirement announcement” (President Fox-Martin), while Q1 introduced “our new CFO, Olivier Leonetti… [who] joined us in March” (President Fox-Martin).
  • Hampton xScale timing evolved: Q4 positioned the lease as “expected in Q1” (CFO Keith Taylor), while Q1 said “our Q1 results do not include the xScale Hampton lease” and the “expanded economics are now included in our guidance for Q2” (CFO Leonetti).
  • Guidance language strengthened: Q4 expected 2026 total revenues to grow “between 9% and 10%” (CFO Taylor), while Q1 raised expected total revenue growth to “10% to 11%” (CFO Leonetti) and lifted EBITDA and AFFO guidance amounts.
  • AI narrative progressed from Q4’s “piloting AI… implement AI across their operations” framing toward Q1’s “enterprise-wide adoption at scale” and “Agentic AI… into distributed deployments” (President Fox-Martin).

Risks and Concerns

  • On energy and pricing volatility, CFO Leonetti said Equinix is “more than 90% hedged for 2026” and expects “minimum impact for 2026.”
  • On geopolitical disruption, President Fox-Martin said DX3’s “RFS date… be impacted due to the conflict,” while emphasizing “our facilities are fully operational.”
  • On capacity constraints, President Fox-Martin said “the availability of power would be the largest constraint,” describing how Equinix may “put some space on hold” to protect broader service obligations.
  • On xScale variability and forecasting, management emphasized deal complexity: “These transactions are always very complex and multifaceted” (President Fox-Martin).

Final Takeaway

Equinix management portrayed Q1 as a record sales-activity quarter with double-digit recurring revenue growth and expanding profitability, while highlighting AI inferencing and “Agentic AI” as drivers of ecosystem demand, Fabric growth, and higher-density deployments. Management raised full-year guidance ranges for total revenue growth, adjusted EBITDA, and AFFO, while emphasizing that Hampton xScale economics shifted into Q2 without changing full-year expectations. The call also surfaced practical constraints—especially power availability—and pointed to continued capacity expansion, hedged energy exposure, and an atNorth acquisition intended to extend long-term development runway in the Nordics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amazon discusses The Apprentice reboot, with Don Jr. as a potential host - WSJ
data.description:
• Amazon (AMZN) is said to be discussing a potential reboot of “The Apprentice,” the reality TV show that once starred Donald Trump and propelled him to national fame. 
  • People familiar with the matter told The Wall Street Journal, that executives at Amazon internally have discussed casting someone very close to President Trump as the host if they were to move ahead with filming new episodes: his oldest son Donald Trump Jr. 
  • “Since our acquisition of MGM, we have had preliminary internal discussions about what’s next for ‘The Apprentice’ as a property,” a spokesperson told the WSJ, but left it at that. Insiders told Variety that the show is currently “not in development” and there have not been “any discussions with anyone outside the company” about producing “The Apprentice” reboot.
  • Talks surrounding the project are still in the early stages, and Amazon executives have not yet approached the Trump family about the project, The WSJ reported.

data.title: Australia’s Q1 export prices slow to 0.5% growth as iron ore weakness weighs; iImport prices increased by 0.1%
West Enclave Merger prices $100M IPO at $10 per share
data.description:
• West Enclave Merger (WENC) priced its IPO of 10M units at a price of $10.00 per unit.
  • The units are expected to commence trading on April 30, 2026, on the NYSE under the symbol "WENC U".
  • Each unit sold in the offering consists of one ordinary share and one right entitling the holder thereof to receive one-tenth of one ordinary share upon the completion of an initial business combination.
  • The ordinary shares and rights are expected to be listed on the NYSE under the symbols "WENC" and "WENC RT", respectively.
  • The underwriters have been granted a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 1.5M units.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about May 1, 2026.

data.title: Procept projects $390M-$410M 2026 revenue as Hydros pricing guides $450K-$460K for rest of year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PROCEPT BioRobotics (PRCT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Over the past 6 months, we have taken decisive actions to reset the organization. We have sharpened our focus on operational excellence, accountability and commercial discipline" (President, CEO & Director Larry Wood).
  • "We completed approximately 12,200 U.S. procedures in the first quarter of 2026" and "we expect the full benefit of these changes to materialize in the second half of 2026" (President Wood).
  • "We sold 49 Hydros systems, which included 2 replacement systems" and "U.S. Hydros system average selling prices of approximately $485,000" (President Wood).
  • "Driven by increased price discipline and better leverage of our cost structure, we delivered first quarter gross margins of 65%" and "we will also continue to manage operating expenses as we work toward achieving positive adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter of 2026" (President Wood).
  • "The EAU updated clinical guidelines to give Aquablation therapy a strong recommendation" and "we also announced the first international launch of Hydros in the U.K." (President Wood).
  • "We have also received FDA clearance on our second-generation FirstAssist AI software" and "we are approaching the completion of patient enrollment in the WATER IV study and based on current trends, expect to be fully enrolled by the end of May" (President Wood).
  • "Total revenue for the first quarter of 2026 was $83.1 million" and "gross margin for the first quarter of 2026 was 65%" (Executive VP & CFO Kevin Waters).

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect full year 2026 total revenue to be in the range of approximately $390 million to $410 million" and "we continue to expect 2026 total U.S. procedures to be in the range of 60,000 to 64,000" (Executive VP Waters).
  • "With respect to new U.S. system pricing, we now expect pricing to range between $450,000 and $460,000 for the remainder of the year" (Executive VP Waters).
  • "We continue to expect full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA loss to be in the range of $30 million to $17 million" and "in the second quarter of 2026, we expect total revenue to be in the range of $91 million to $95 million" (Executive VP Waters).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s framing, management reiterated the same full-year 2026 revenue and procedure ranges first introduced after "resetting" guidance in Q4, when it said it was "resetting 2026 guidance to $390 million to $410 million" (President Wood, Q4 2025).

Financial results

  • "U.S. revenue for the quarter was $72 million" and "international revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $11.1 million" (Executive VP Waters).
  • "Total U.S. handpiece and other consumable revenue was $43 million" and "total U.S. system revenue was $23.4 million" (Executive VP Waters).
  • "Total operating expenses for the first quarter of 2026 were $86.6 million" and "net loss for the first quarter of 2026 was $31.6 million" (Executive VP Waters).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA was a loss of $18.1 million" and "cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash totaled $249 million as of March 31, 2026" (Executive VP Waters).
  • On profitability trajectory and margins, management linked Q1’s 65% gross margin to prior-quarter volatility: "In the fourth quarter of 2025, we reported gross margins of 61% and indicated this level was temporary" (President Wood).

Q&A

  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: "the strength on the capital side" and "the ASP benefit"; President Wood: "we did certainly implement pricing discipline" and "we expect the momentum on procedures to build throughout the quarter -- sorry, throughout the year."
  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: "the confidence in the -- especially the back half"; Executive VP Waters: "our confidence in executing within our guidance range this year has increased with our Q1 performance" and "it will probably be the end of Q2 where we start to formally update some metrics around pricing and volume."
  • Nathan Treybeck, Wells Fargo: "procedures were flat quarter-over-quarter" and "any underlying softening in demand"; President Wood: "No" and "we had strong system placements" that "we think" will contribute "as we get deeper in the year."
  • Nathan Treybeck, Wells Fargo: "handpiece sales were below your target of 1: 1"; President Wood: "we still remain very confident" in "the 1:1 full year ratio" and "the number that we're focused on are procedures."
  • Michael Kratky, Leerink: "Was that the full year average? Or was that for the remainder of the year?"; Executive VP Waters: "for the remainder of the year" and "that puts the full year average more towards the upper end, probably around $460,000."
  • Richard Newitter, Truist: "where we should expect to see the proof points"; President Wood: "we have a lot of confidence in our launch teams" and "there's always just going to be a lag between driving patient awareness and a patient getting a procedure."
  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: "potential for Aquablation procedure to kind of move up in the APC level"; President Wood: "We haven't built that into our modeling" and "If that happened, it would be certainly an upside to reimbursement."
  • Christopher Pasquale, Nephron Research: "I'd love to hear a little bit more about the U.K. opportunity"; President Wood: "in the first quarter, we sold 7 new Hydros systems in the United Kingdom" and "we continue to evaluate what other markets in Europe that we should be looking at."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on procedure cadence and back-half weighting, including "procedures were flat quarter-over-quarter" (Nathan Treybeck, Wells Fargo) and "the midpoint of $93 million is a little below the Street" (Stephanie Piazzola, BofA).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A, using confidence qualifiers alongside caution, including "we remain confident" (President Wood) and "it's prudent to maintain current expectations" (Executive VP Waters).
  • Versus last quarter, management’s tone appeared less defensive around inventory mechanics and more focused on execution sustainability; in Q4 it stressed foundational change, including "we are now resetting 2026 guidance" (President Wood, Q4 2025).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Pricing and commercialization were central in both calls; Q1 emphasized that "U.S. Hydros system average selling prices of approximately $485,000" was "an all-time high" (President Wood), while Q4 highlighted system ASP of "approximately $425,000" (Executive VP Waters, Q4 2025).
  • Handpiece normalization messaging shifted from Q4’s disruption ("handpiece unit sales were approximately 80% of procedures," President Wood, Q4 2025) to Q1’s steadier relationship ("handpieces sold representing approximately 95% of procedures," President Wood).
  • Q1 introduced new external catalysts not discussed in Q4: the EAU "strong recommendation" guideline update and the "first international launch of Hydros in the U.K." (President Wood).

Risks and concerns

  • "resulted in some short-term disruption in the first quarter" from the commercial realignment, with benefits expected later: "we expect the benefits to build as the organization settles into the new model" (President Wood).
  • Margin sensitivity to trade policy: "This includes $5 million to $6 million of tariff expense" and "gross margin guidance does not reflect any potential benefit from previously paid tariff refunds" (Executive VP Waters).
  • Uncertainty in procedure-to-handpiece quarterly alignment: "handpiece sales are always going to fluctuate" even as management reiterated full-year expectations (President Wood).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as early evidence of its operational reset, highlighting $83.1 million in revenue, 65% gross margin, higher U.S. system and handpiece pricing, and continued full-year guidance of $390 million to $410 million in revenue and 60,000 to 64,000 U.S. procedures, while positioning the commercial reorganization, launch-team execution, U.K. Hydros expansion, and WATER IV enrollment timing as key 2026 drivers with an explicit target of positive adjusted EBITDA in Q4 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan economic update: Retail sales surge 1.7% on stimulus while industrial output stumbles
data.description:
Japan’s economy presented a stark contrast in March 2026, as resilient consumer spending fueled by government stimulus offset a unexpected contraction in industrial activity. While retail sales comfortably beat market expectations.

Retail sales in Japan rose by 1.7% year-over-year in March 2026, rebounding from a revised 0.1% decline in February and exceeding market predictions of 0.8%. This increase reflects the government's stimulus efforts to boost spending. 

On a monthly basis, retail trade grew 1.3%, rebounding from a 2.0% fall in February.

Meanwhile, industrial production fell by 0.5% month-on-month, below the 1.1% expected, marking a second consecutive decline due to external challenges.

On an annual basis, output increased 2.3%, picking up from a 0.4% rise in February and marking the fastest pace since June 2025.

The Nikkei 225 Index dropped 1% to around 59,300, while the broader Topix Index fell 1.6% to 3,710 in post-holiday trading on Thursday. The Japanese yen weakened past the critical 160 per dollar threshold on Thursday, reaching its lowest level since July 2024.

data.title: Standard Chartered tops forecasts, downplays Gulf conflict risks
data.description:
UK's Standard Chartered reported a strong Q1 performance with pretax profit of about $2.45 billion, up roughly 17% Y/Y and ahead of forecasts of $2.14B. Its EPS was 0.742, up 31% Y/Y

The beat was driven by strong net new money inflows into its wealth management business, which has become one of the bank’s key growth engines across Asia, the Middle East, and Africa.

Net interest income rose 1% to $2.9B, while non-interest income climbed 16% to $3B, driven by strong growth in Wealth Solutions and Global Banking.

Wealth Solutions posted a record quarter, with income up 32% on robust investment products and bancassurance, while Global Banking rose 19% on higher deal activity and capital markets volumes.

The company kept its 2026 outlook unchanged: operating income is expected to grow at the lower end of the 5%–7% range Y/Y; cost expected to be flat, while its statutory RoTE greater than 12%.

"Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and global economic uncertainty, our advantaged market presence and disciplined risk management give us confidence in our ability to perform. We continue to support our clients as they manage their businesses and wealth across borders, and we look forward to setting out our next phase of growth at our Investor Event next month." said Bill Winters CEO.
